content:
# PRESSURE TRANSIENT ANALYSIS OF DATA FROM PERMANENT DOWNHOLE GAUGES

**Summary**: The thesis investigates the application of pressure transient analysis (PTA) on data acquired from permanent downhole gauges (PDHG) in horizontal wells. It details a workflow for automated data processing, including mirroring, wavelet-based denoising, and shut-in detection, to evaluate reservoir parameters like permeability and skin factor.

---

## Research Problem

The primary challenge is the lack of established workflows to transform massive amounts of raw, often noisy, real-time data from PDHMS into actionable reservoir characterization information. Specifically, it addresses how to handle issues like data interruptions, long stabilization periods, and minimum duration requirements in a dynamic environment.

## Objectives

- Develop a workflow for efficient utilization of the real-time data for pressure transient analysis.
- Assess the rate effects on establishing reliable analysis (minimum duration, long stabilization, and data interruptions).
- Develop an automated workflow for processing raw PDHG data including mirroring, denoising, and filtering.
- Implement automatic shut-in detection and alarm systems for real-time monitoring.
- Evaluate the impact of data interruptions (e.g., frozen data or missing early-time data) on the determination of well and reservoir parameters.
- Perform pressure transient analysis on horizontal wells in the Unayzah formation to determine permeability, skin factor, and wellbore storage.

## Methods

The study utilizes a multi-step workflow for PDHG data: 1. Mirroring: Importing raw high-frequency pressure and flow rate data into a system where it is indexed and stored as binary files. 2. Data Reduction: Using a wavelet-based algorithm to denoise and filter the data, removing outliers while preserving significant events like shut-ins. 3. Event Detection/Alarming: Setting logical formulas to trigger alerts for abnormal pressure or flow rates. 4. Shut-in Detection: Utilizing manual, semi-automatic (click-to-select), and fully automatic algorithms based on pressure behavior. 5. Analysis: Exporting cleaned data into a well test analysis package to generate log-log and semi-log plots for calculating parameters like $k_h$, $k_v/k_r$, skin factor, and wellbore storage.

## Data and Materials

The study analyzes data from three wells: Y-2 (1,800 hours of data with 12,541 pressure points), Z-3 (5,500 hours of data with 46,367 pressure points), and A-105 (comparison well). Well Y-2 is in the Arab-D reservoir; Well Z-3 is in the Hadriya reservoir.

## Key Findings

- The study identifies that the full benefits of PDHMS investment are only realized when data is moved from simple monitoring to a source for reservoir characterization. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 15]])
- Wavelet filtration can reduce the number of raw data points by a factor of 100 to 1,000 while preserving critical high-frequency information like shut-in events. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 37]])
- A time step of 10 to 20 seconds is identified as a good compromise for initial wavelet filtration to balance computational demand and the ability to capture pressure transients. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 38]])
- The study highlights that multi-phase flow meters (MPFM) coupled with PDHMS provide significant value in translating data into reservoir characterization information. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 16]])
- Case Y-2 analysis included a comparison of pre- and post-stimulation log-log analysis to evaluate well performance. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 73]])
- The wavelet-based algorithm for data reduction effectively removes noise while preserving significant events like shut-ins by providing an adjustable band around the data set. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 42]])
- Automatic shut-in detection can be performed using three methods: manual, semi-automatic (click to select), and fully automatic based on pressure behavior/shape. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 45]])
- Well X-1 in the Unayzah-A reservoir was characterized with a permeability thickness product ($k_h 	imes h$) of 785 md.ft and an average permeability ($k_{avg}$) of 8.72 md. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 65]])
- The skin factor for well X-1 was calculated as -2.89, potentially attributed to georeserves or fractures since no prior stimulation was conducted. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 65]])
- Data loss during the early time of a pressure buildup (BUP3) significantly masks well-related parameters like skin and wellbore storage (WBS). ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 72]])
- Data loss in the middle time of a pressure buildup impacts the determination of $k_h$ more than well-related parameters or boundary effects. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 72]])
- Data loss in the late time of a pressure buildup affects only the determination of reservoir boundaries. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 72]])
- For well Y-2, acid treatment resulted in an effective horizontal length Le of 1,816 ft (compared to 1,286 ft pre-acid) and a skin factor of -2.4 (compared to -0.1). ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 70]])
- The productivity index for well Y-2 more than doubled from 20.2 BPD/psi to 49 BPD/psi after acid treatment. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 70]])
- Well Y-2's flow capacity was determined to be 10,600 md.ft with a permeability of 96.1 md. ([[2011-alburaikan|AlBuraikan, 2011, p. 70]])

## Limitations

The analysis of $k_z$ and $k_x$ is often non-unique or requires iterative estimation due to missing information on wellbore length ($L_w$) or reservoir dimensions. Data interruptions (frozen data) can significantly impact the accuracy of specific parameters like skin factor.

## Recommendations and Future Work

Not stated in source
